title: Remove occurrences of x
description: Remove all occurrences of x from the array in place and return new length

examples.explanation: Initial: arr = [3, 2, 2, 3], n = 4, x = 3, i = 0. Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=3 equals x. Shift left: copy arr[1]=2 to arr[0], arr[2]=2 to arr[1], arr[3]=3 to arr[2]. arr becomes [2, 2, 3, 3], n decrements to 3. Do not increment i (stay at 0). Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=2 not equal x, increment i to 1. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 not equal x, increment i to 2. Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=3 equals x. Shift left: copy arr[3]=3 to arr[2]. arr becomes [2, 2, 3, 3], n decrements to 2. Do not increment i (stay at 2). Step 5: i=2, but n=2, so i < n is false, exit loop. Final: new length = 2, arr first 2 elements are [2, 2].

Initial: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], n = 5, x = 6, i = 0. Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1 not equal x, increment i to 1. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2 not equal x, increment i to 2. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=3 not equal x, increment i to 3. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=4 not equal x, increment i to 4. Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=5 not equal x, increment i to 5. Step 6: i=5, i < n is false, exit loop. No shifts occur. Final: new length = 5, arr unchanged.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force with Shifting
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array, and whenever x is found, shift all subsequent elements left by one position to fill the gap. This requires nested loops and is inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove All Occurrences of x Using Brute Force Shifting
ladders.desc: Complete in-place removal of all occurrences of x from an array using nested loops and shifting.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force shifting approach. The intuition is straightforward: we need to eliminate all instances of x from the array while maintaining the relative order of other elements. Since we must do this in-place (without using extra space for another array), we use a two-pointer-like technique with nested loops. The outer loop iterates through each position in the array. When we find x at index i, we need to remove it. To do this, we use an inner loop that shifts all elements from i+1 to the end left by one position, effectively overwriting x. This creates a gap at the end, so we decrement the array length. However, after shifting, the element now at index i might be another x (if it was originally at i+1), so we must re-check index i by not incrementing i in that iteration. This ensures we catch consecutive x's. The algorithm continues until we've processed all original elements. The time complexity is O(n²) in the worst case (when all elements are x, each removal requires shifting nearly n elements, and there are n removals). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is inefficient for large arrays due to the quadratic time, but it's conceptually simple and demonstrates in-place modification without extra memory. Common mistakes include: forgetting to re-check index i after shifting (leading to missed consecutive x's), incorrect loop bounds causing index errors, or not updating the length properly. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the key subroutine of shifting elements into the full solution.

examples.trace: Initial: arr = [3, 2, 2, 3], n = 4, x = 3, i = 0. Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=3 equals x. Shift left: copy arr[1]=2 to arr[0], arr[2]=2 to arr[1], arr[3]=3 to arr[2]. arr becomes [2, 2, 3, 3], n decrements to 3. Do not increment i (stay at 0). Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=2 not equal x, increment i to 1. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 not equal x, increment i to 2. Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=3 equals x. Shift left: copy arr[3]=3 to arr[2]. arr becomes [2, 2, 3, 3], n decrements to 2. Do not increment i (stay at 2). Step 5: i=2, but n=2, so i < n is false, exit loop. Final: new length = 2, arr first 2 elements are [2, 2].

Initial: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], n = 5, x = 6, i = 0. Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1 not equal x, increment i to 1. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2 not equal x, increment i to 2. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=3 not equal x, increment i to 3. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=4 not equal x, increment i to 4. Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=5 not equal x, increment i to 5. Step 6: i=5, i < n is false, exit loop. No shifts occur. Final: new length = 5, arr unchanged.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Shift Elements Left from a Given Index
ladders.desc: Shift all elements from a start index to the end left by one position.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach. The intuition is that when we remove an element at index i, we need to close the gap by moving every element after i one step to the left. This overwrites the element at i with the element at i+1, then i+1 with i+2, and so on, effectively 'shifting' the subarray. The algorithm takes an array, its current length n, and a start index i. We use a loop from j = i to n-2 (since we're accessing j+1, we stop at the second-to-last element) and set arr[j] = arr[j+1]. After the loop, the last element (at index n-1) becomes duplicated (it's now at n-2 as well), but we decrement n to ignore it. The time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements shifted (up to n in worst case). Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called whenever x is found. Common mistakes: incorrect loop bounds leading to index out of bounds, forgetting to decrement n, or shifting in the wrong direction. The connection to L2 is that this subroutine implements the core logic of in-place removal via shifting.

examples.trace: Initial: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], n=5, i=1. Loop j from 1 to 3: j=1: arr[1] = arr[2] -> arr becomes [1, 3, 3, 4, 5]. j=2: arr[2] = arr[3] -> [1, 3, 4, 4, 5]. j=3: arr[3] = arr[4] -> [1, 3, 4, 5, 5]. Decrement n to 4. Final: n=4, arr first 4 elements are [1, 3, 4, 5].

Initial: arr = [10, 20, 30], n=3, i=0. Loop j from 0 to 1: j=0: arr[0] = arr[1] -> arr becomes [20, 20, 30]. j=1: arr[1] = arr[2] -> [20, 30, 30]. Decrement n to 2. Final: n=2, arr first 2 elements are [20, 30].

testCases.input: arr = [a, b, c, d], n = 4, i = 1

ladders.title: Core Logic — In-place Removal by Overwriting with Shifts
ladders.desc: The technique of removing an element by shifting subsequent elements left.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight of the brute force approach: how to remove an element from an array in-place without using extra space. The intuition is that arrays in memory are contiguous blocks; we cannot 'delete' an element directly, but we can overwrite it by moving later elements forward. When we find x at index i, we shift all elements from i+1 to the end left by one. This does two things: it copies the element at i+1 to i (overwriting x), and it moves every other element one step left, leaving a 'gap' at the end (the last element is now duplicated). By reducing the logical length of the array (e.g., with a variable n), we ignore that duplicated element. The key is that this maintains order of non-x elements. The algorithm involves a loop to identify x and a call to the shift subroutine. Time is O(n²) due to potential repeated shifts. Space is O(1). This builds on L3's array traversal and feeds into L1's subroutine implementation. Common mistakes: not handling consecutive x's correctly (after shifting, index i may have a new x, so we must re-check), or off-by-one errors in shift indices. The connection to L3 is that this logic uses array indexing and loops to implement the removal.

examples.trace: Initial: arr = [3, 4, 5], n=3. Find x=3 at i=0. Shift left from i=0: arr[0] = arr[1] -> [4, 4, 5]; arr[1] = arr[2] -> [4, 5, 5]. Decrement n to 2. Final: first 2 elements are [4, 5].

Initial: arr = [1, 2, 2, 3], n=4. Find x=2 at i=1. Shift left from i=1: arr[1] = arr[2] -> [1, 2, 2, 3]; arr[2] = arr[3] -> [1, 2, 3, 3]. Decrement n to 3. Now i=1, arr[1]=2 is x again (consecutive), so repeat: shift left from i=1: arr[1] = arr[2] -> [1, 3, 3, 3]; arr[2] = arr[3] -> [1, 3, 3, 3]. Decrement n to 2. Final: first 2 elements are [1, 3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing an Array to Find an Element
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and check each element against a target value.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for the brute force approach: we need to scan the array to locate occurrences of x. The intuition is simple: arrays are ordered collections, so we examine each position sequentially using an index. The algorithm uses a loop (e.g., for or while) with an index variable i that goes from 0 to n-1, where n is the array length. At each step, we compare arr[i] to x. If equal, we've found a match and can take action (like removal). The time complexity is O(n) for the traversal itself. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to identify where shifts are needed. Common mistakes: incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one errors), modifying the array while iterating without adjusting the index (which can skip elements), or using the wrong comparison operator. The connection to L4 is that this building block relies on basic operations like indexing and comparison. In the full problem, after shifting, we may not increment i to re-check the same index, which is a nuance beyond simple traversal.

examples.trace: Initial: i=0, arr[0]=1 != 3, increment i to 1. i=1, arr[1]=2 != 3, increment i to 2. i=2, arr[2]=3 == 3, found. Stop or continue depending on goal.
Initial: i=0, arr[0]=5 != 8, increment i to 1. i=1, arr[1]=6 != 8, increment i to 2. i=2, arr[2]=7 != 8, increment i to 3. i=3 >= n=3, exit loop, not found.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Read from and write to specific positions in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is crucial for array manipulation. Arrays are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via an index (usually starting at 0). The intuition is that an index acts like an address: arr[i] gives the value at position i. We can read this value (e.g., to compare with x) or modify it (e.g., during shifting). In pseudocode, use arr[i] to access. For modification, assign a value: arr[i] = newValue. This is an O(1) operation time-wise. Space is O(1) per access. This feeds into L3 as traversal relies on accessing elements, and shifting involves both reading and writing. Common mistakes: using indices out of bounds (e.g., i >= length), confusing zero-based indexing, or incorrect assignment syntax. The connection to L5 is that this operation assumes understanding of arrays as indexed collections. In the brute force approach, we use this to overwrite elements during shifts.

examples.trace: Initial arr = [10, 20, 30]. Read arr[1]: index 1 corresponds to second element, value is 20. Set arr[0] = 5: assign 5 to first element, so arr becomes [5, 20, 30].

examples.input: arr = [1, 2], read arr[0], set arr[1] = arr[0]
examples.trace: Initial arr = [1, 2]. Read arr[0]: value is 1. Set arr[1] = arr[0]: assign value at index 0 (1) to index 1, so arr becomes [1, 1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Indexed Collections
ladders.desc: Basic idea of arrays: ordered lists of elements accessible by position.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for the problem. An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type. The intuition is like a row of boxes: each box holds a value and has a number (index) starting from 0. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], index 0 has 10, index 1 has 20, index 2 has 30. We can think of it as a list where position matters. Arrays allow fast access to any element if you know its index. Key properties: elements are contiguous in memory, size is fixed (though we can simulate resizing), and indices are integers. This foundation is needed for L4's operations. Common beginner mistakes: thinking indices start at 1, confusing arrays with other collections like lists, or not understanding that arrays have a length. In the context of the problem, we use arrays to hold the input and modify it in-place. The connection to higher levels is that all array manipulations build on this basic concept.

examples.trace: Visualize as boxes: Box 0 contains 5, Box 1 contains 10, Box 2 contains 15. The array has length 3. To get the first element, use index 0. To get the last, use index 2 (length-1).
Single-element array: only one box at index 0 with value 100. Length is 1. Accessing index 0 gives 100; any other index (e.g., 1) is invalid.

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c], what is at index 2?

approaches.name: Approach 2: Two Pointer In-Place
approaches.summary: Use two pointers: one to iterate through the array and another to track the position for non-x elements. Copy non-x elements to the front, overwriting x occurrences without shifting entire subarrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove All Occurrences of x In-Place
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two-pointer technique to remove all occurrences of x from array in O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer in-place approach elegantly solves the problem by maintaining two indices: one to read through the original array and another to write non-x elements. The intuition is that we can overwrite x occurrences as we traverse, avoiding expensive shifting operations. We initialize a write pointer at index 0 to track where the next non-x element should go. As we iterate with a read pointer from 0 to n-1, we check each element: if it's not x, we copy it to the write position and increment both pointers; if it is x, we only increment the read pointer, effectively skipping it. This ensures all non-x elements are compacted at the front while x elements are overwritten. The final value of the write pointer gives the new length. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array once, and space complexity is O(1) as we modify the array in place without extra data structures. This connects directly to the key subroutine of filtering elements. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, incorrectly updating pointers, or misunderstanding that elements beyond the new length may contain old values. The algorithm is efficient and widely applicable to similar filtering problems.

examples.trace: Initialize write = 0. Read i=0: arr[0]=3 equals x=3, skip (write=0, arr unchanged). i=1: arr[1]=2 not equal to x, copy to arr[write]=arr[0], arr becomes [2, 2, 3, 3], increment write=1. i=2: arr[2]=2 not equal to x, copy to arr[1], arr becomes [2, 2, 3, 3], write=2. i=3: arr[3]=3 equals x, skip. End: new_length = write = 2.

write=0. i=0: arr[0]=0 not x, copy to arr[0], arr unchanged [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2], write=1. i=1: arr[1]=1 not x, copy to arr[1], arr unchanged, write=2. i=2: arr[2]=2 equals x, skip. i=3: arr[3]=2 equals x, skip. i=4: arr[4]=3 not x, copy to arr[2], arr becomes [0,1,3,2,3,0,4,2], write=3. i=5: arr[5]=0 not x, copy to arr[3], arr becomes [0,1,3,0,3,0,4,2], write=4. i=6: arr[6]=4 not x, copy to arr[4], arr becomes [0,1,3,0,4,0,4,2], write=5. i=7: arr[7]=2 equals x, skip. End: new_length = 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Filter Array Elements In-Place
ladders.desc: Implement a function that filters an array by copying non-target elements to the front using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full problem, focusing on the two-pointer mechanism to filter elements. The intuition is to separate the array into two parts: the front containing filtered elements and the rest. We use a write pointer to mark the next available position for a non-target element. As we iterate with a read pointer, we check each element: if it passes the filter condition (not equal to x), we copy it to the write position and advance both pointers; otherwise, we only advance the read pointer. This efficiently compacts valid elements without shifting. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass, and space is O(1) as we modify in place. This feeds into the full problem by providing the core filtering logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in pointer updates or not initializing pointers correctly. The pseudocode: initialize write = 0; for i from 0 to n-1: if arr[i] != x: arr[write] = arr[i]; write += 1; return write. This pattern is reusable for various filtering tasks.

examples.trace: write=0. i=0: arr[0]=1 not 2, copy to arr[0], arr unchanged [1,2,3,4], write=1. i=1: arr[1]=2 equals 2, skip. i=2: arr[2]=3 not 2, copy to arr[1], arr becomes [1,3,3,4], write=2. i=3: arr[3]=4 not 2, copy to arr[2], arr becomes [1,3,4,4], write=3. End: new_length = 3.
write=0. i=0: arr[0]=5 equals 5, skip. i=1: arr[1]=5 equals 5, skip. i=2: arr[2]=5 equals 5, skip. End: new_length = 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Compaction Technique
ladders.desc: Learn how to use two indices to compact valid elements to the front of an array in one pass.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves using two pointers to traverse and modify an array simultaneously. The write pointer tracks where to place the next valid element, while the read pointer scans each element. When the read pointer encounters a valid element, it copies it to the write pointer's position, and both pointers advance; if invalid, only the read pointer advances. This compacts valid elements to the front without extra space. The intuition is that we're building a new filtered array in place, overwriting invalid positions. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan, and space is O(1). This technique feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the mechanical process. Common mistakes include confusing pointer roles or not handling edge cases like all invalid elements. Pseudocode: write = 0; for read from 0 to n-1: if condition(arr[read]): arr[write] = arr[read]; write += 1. This pattern is fundamental for in-place operations.

examples.trace: write=0. read=0: arr[0]=0 invalid, skip. read=1: arr[1]=1 valid, copy to arr[0], arr becomes [1,1,0,2,3], write=1. read=2: arr[2]=0 invalid, skip. read=3: arr[3]=2 valid, copy to arr[1], arr becomes [1,2,0,2,3], write=2. read=4: arr[4]=3 valid, copy to arr[2], arr becomes [1,2,3,2,3], write=3. End: new_length = 3.

write=0. read=0: arr[0]=1 odd invalid, skip. read=1: arr[1]=2 even valid, copy to arr[0], arr becomes [2,2,3,4,5], write=1. read=2: arr[2]=3 odd invalid, skip. read=3: arr[3]=4 even valid, copy to arr[1], arr becomes [2,4,3,4,5], write=2. read=4: arr[4]=5 odd invalid, skip. End: new_length = 2.

testCases.input: Compact true values in [true, false, true]

ladders.title: Building Block — Conditional Copying in Arrays
ladders.desc: Practice copying array elements based on a condition without using extra space.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on selectively copying elements within the same array. The intuition is to iterate through the array and, for each element, decide whether to copy it to a new position based on a condition. We maintain a destination index that increments only when a copy occurs. This teaches how to modify arrays in place without auxiliary storage. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration, and space is O(1). It connects to the core logic by introducing the copy mechanism. Common mistakes include overwriting elements before they're read or incorrect index management. Pseudocode: dest = 0; for src from 0 to n-1: if condition(arr[src]): arr[dest] = arr[src]; dest += 1. This skill is essential for efficient array manipulations.

examples.input: Copy odd numbers to front in [1, 2, 3, 4]
examples.trace: dest=0. src=0: arr[0]=1 odd, copy to arr[0], arr unchanged [1,2,3,4], dest=1. src=1: arr[1]=2 even, skip. src=2: arr[2]=3 odd, copy to arr[1], arr becomes [1,3,3,4], dest=2. src=3: arr[3]=4 even, skip. End: new_length = dest = 2.

dest=0. src=0: arr[0]=5 not >10, skip. src=1: arr[1]=15 >10, copy to arr[0], arr becomes [15,15,10,20], dest=1. src=2: arr[2]=10 not >10, skip. src=3: arr[3]=20 >10, copy to arr[1], arr becomes [15,20,10,20], dest=2. End: new_length = 2.

testCases.input: Copy even numbers to front in [3, 4, 5, 6]
Copy non-empty strings to front in ['', 'a', '', 'b']
Copy positive numbers to front in [-1, 0, 1]
Copy multiples of 5 to front in [10, 3, 15, 7]
Copy boolean true to front in [false, true, false]
Copy elements != null to front in [null, 'x', null]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate and Compare Array Elements
ladders.desc: Learn to loop through an array and compare each element to a target value.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing an array from start to end and performing a comparison on each element. The intuition is to examine every item sequentially to make decisions, which is foundational for array processing. We use a simple for-loop or while-loop to access each index, compare the element to a target (e.g., using ==, !=, <, >), and potentially take action based on the result. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear traversal, and space is O(1) if no extra data is stored. This feeds into the building block by providing the iteration and comparison skills. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds or incorrect comparison operators. Pseudocode: for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i] == target: # do something. This operation is crucial for many algorithms.

examples.input: Iterate and count occurrences of 2 in [1, 2, 2, 3]
examples.trace: Initialize count=0. i=0: arr[0]=1 !=2, count=0. i=1: arr[1]=2 ==2, count=1. i=2: arr[2]=2 ==2, count=2. i=3: arr[3]=3 !=2, count=2. End: count = 2.

examples.input: Iterate and find if 5 exists in [3, 4, 6]
examples.trace: Initialize found=false. i=0: arr[0]=3 !=5, found=false. i=1: arr[1]=4 !=5, found=false. i=2: arr[2]=6 !=5, found=false. End: found = false.

testCases.input: Iterate and compare to true in [false, true, false]
Iterate and compare to null in [null, 1, null]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Assignment
ladders.desc: Understand how to access and modify elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of arrays: they are ordered collections where each element has an index starting from 0. To access an element, use the syntax arr[index]; to modify it, use arr[index] = value. The intuition is that arrays provide direct access to any position, making them efficient for sequential processing. Key concepts include zero-based indexing, bounds checking (indices must be within 0 to length-1), and that assignment overwrites the existing value. Time complexity for access/assignment is O(1), and space is O(1) per operation. This feeds into the basic operation by enabling element manipulation. Common mistakes include using indices out of bounds or confusing index values. Examples: arr = [10, 20, 30]; arr[0] accesses 10; arr[1] = 25 changes the array to [10, 25, 30]. This is essential for all array-based algorithms.

examples.input: Array arr = [5, 10, 15]. Access arr[1], then set arr[2] = 20.
examples.trace: Start: arr = [5, 10, 15]. Access arr[1]: index 1 corresponds to second element, value is 10. Set arr[2] = 20: index 2 is third element, change it from 15 to 20. End: arr = [5, 10, 20].

examples.input: Array arr = []. Try to access arr[0].
examples.trace: Start: arr = [] with length 0. Access arr[0]: index 0 is requested, but valid indices are from 0 to length-1 = -1, so no valid indices exist. This causes an out-of-bounds error.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Count and Replace
approaches.summary: First count all non-x elements, then iterate again to place them at the correct positions from the start. This separates counting and placement into two distinct phases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove All Occurrences of x In-Place
ladders.desc: Complete solution using the Count and Replace approach to remove all x from array in O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation:
The Count and Replace approach solves the problem in two distinct phases, separating counting from placement. This separation makes the algorithm intuitive and easy to reason about. First, we count how many elements are NOT x - this tells us exactly how many elements will remain after removal, and also gives us the new length to return. Second, we iterate through the array again, but this time we only copy non-x elements to the front of the array, using the count we computed as a guide for where to place them. The intuition is that by first counting, we know exactly how many valid elements exist, which eliminates any uncertainty about where to place elements during the second pass. The algorithm works because we're essentially compacting the array: all non-x elements get moved to the front, preserving their original order, while the positions after the new length can contain any values (they're effectively ignored). The two-phase nature ensures we never overwrite elements we might need later, since we only write to positions we've already processed or are part of the final result. Time complexity is O(2n) = O(n) since we make two passes through the array. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is particularly good for interview settings because it's easy to explain and has clear separation of concerns.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. First pass: Count all elements that are NOT x
   For each element in array:
       if element != x: count += 1
3. Initialize write_index = 0
4. Second pass: Copy non-x elements to front
   For each element in array:
       if element != x:
           array[write_index] = element
           write_index += 1
5. Return count (which equals write_index)

Connection to L1: This full solution uses the counting subroutine from L1 and the placement logic from the core technique.

Common mistakes:
1. Trying to do counting and placement in one pass - this often leads to overwriting elements
2. Forgetting that the array should be modified in-place
3. Returning the wrong value (should return count, not write_index or array length)
4. Not preserving order of non-x elements

Real code pattern:
def remove_occurrences(arr, x):
    count = 0
    for num in arr:
        if num != x:
            count += 1
    
    write_idx = 0
    for num in arr:
        if num != x:
            arr[write_idx] = num
            write_idx += 1
    
    return count

examples.trace:
Step 1: Count non-3 elements
   arr = [3, 2, 2, 3]
   count = 0
   Check 3: equal to x, skip → count = 0
   Check 2: not equal → count = 1
   Check 2: not equal → count = 2
   Check 3: equal to x, skip → count = 2

Step 2: Place non-3 elements at front
   arr = [3, 2, 2, 3], write_idx = 0
   Check 3: equal to x, skip
   Check 2: not equal → arr[0] = 2, write_idx = 1
   Check 2: not equal → arr[1] = 2, write_idx = 2
   Check 3: equal to x, skip
   Final arr = [2, 2, 2, 3] (first 2 positions valid)

Return count = 2

Step 1: Count non-2 elements
   arr = [0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 0, 4, 2]
   count = 0
   0 ≠ 2 → count = 1
   1 ≠ 2 → count = 2
   2 = 2 → skip
   2 = 2 → skip
   3 ≠ 2 → count = 3
   0 ≠ 2 → count = 4
   4 ≠ 2 → count = 5
   2 = 2 → skip
   count = 5

Step 2: Place non-2 elements at front
   arr = [0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 0, 4, 2], write_idx = 0
   0 ≠ 2 → arr[0] = 0, write_idx = 1
   1 ≠ 2 → arr[1] = 1, write_idx = 2
   2 = 2 → skip
   2 = 2 → skip
   3 ≠ 2 → arr[2] = 3, write_idx = 3
   0 ≠ 2 → arr[3] = 0, write_idx = 4
   4 ≠ 2 → arr[4] = 4, write_idx = 5
   2 = 2 → skip
   Final arr = [0, 1, 3, 0, 4, 0, 4, 2] (first 5 positions valid)

Return count = 5

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Count Non-x Elements
ladders.desc: Count how many elements in an array are NOT equal to a given value x.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the first phase of the Count and Replace approach. The intuition is simple: we need to know exactly how many elements will remain after removing all x's. By counting non-x elements first, we establish the target size of our final array and know exactly how many positions we need to fill. This counting phase is crucial because it gives us complete information before we start modifying the array. Without this count, we wouldn't know where to stop writing elements in the second phase. The algorithm is straightforward: iterate through each element, check if it's not equal to x, and increment a counter if true. This is essentially a filtered count operation. Time complexity is O(n) for scanning the array once. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a counter variable. This subroutine feeds directly into the full solution by providing the count that becomes both the return value and the guide for the placement phase.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. For each element in array:
   a. If element != x:
        count += 1
3. Return count

Connection to L0: This count becomes the return value in the full problem and determines how many elements need placement.

Common mistakes:
1. Counting x instead of non-x elements
2. Not initializing count to 0
3. Using == instead of != in the comparison
4. Forgetting to return the count

Real code pattern:
def count_non_x(arr, x):
    count = 0
    for num in arr:
        if num != x:
            count += 1
    return count

examples.trace:
count = 0
Check 1: 1 ≠ 3 → count = 1
Check 2: 2 ≠ 3 → count = 2
Check 3: 3 = 3 → skip
Check 4: 4 ≠ 3 → count = 3
Check 5: 5 ≠ 3 → count = 4
Return 4

count = 0
Check 5: 5 = 5 → skip
Check 5: 5 = 5 → skip
Check 5: 5 = 5 → skip
Check 5: 5 = 5 → skip
Check 5: 5 = 5 → skip
Return 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pass Array Compaction
ladders.desc: The technique of making two passes over an array: one to gather information, another to act on it.
ladders.explanation:
The core insight of the Count and Replace approach is the separation of information gathering (counting) from action (placement). This two-pass strategy is powerful because it eliminates complex in-place modification logic that can lead to errors. In the first pass, we safely gather all necessary information without modifying the array. In this case, we count non-x elements. This count tells us exactly how many valid elements exist and where they should end up. The second pass then uses this information to perform the actual modification. Because we know exactly how many elements need to be placed, we can use a simple write pointer that starts at 0 and increments only when we find a valid element. The intuition is similar to having a blueprint before construction: we survey the land (first pass) to know what we're working with, then build according to plan (second pass). This approach guarantees we never overwrite elements we might need later, since all writes happen at or ahead of the current read position. Time complexity is O(2n) = O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This technique connects to L1 by using the count from the first pass to control the second pass.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. First pass: Gather information (e.g., count non-x elements)
2. Initialize write pointer based on gathered information
3. Second pass: Read each element and write valid ones to array[write_pointer], incrementing pointer only for valid elements

Connection to L0: This is the fundamental technique that makes the full solution work correctly and efficiently.

Common mistakes:
1. Trying to combine both passes into one (often leads to overwriting)
2. Not resetting the write pointer properly
3. Writing to wrong indices in second pass
4. Not understanding why two passes are necessary

Real code pattern:
def two_pass_compaction(arr, x):
    # First pass: count
    count = 0
    for num in arr:
        if num != x:
            count += 1
    
    # Second pass: place
    write_idx = 0
    for num in arr:
        if num != x:
            arr[write_idx] = num
            write_idx += 1
    
    return count

examples.input: arr = [A, B, A, C, A], remove A
examples.output: 2 (and arr becomes [B, C, A, C, A])
examples.trace:
First pass (count non-A):
   Check A: skip
   Check B: count = 1
   Check A: skip
   Check C: count = 2
   Check A: skip
   count = 2

Second pass (place non-A):
   write_idx = 0
   Check A: skip
   Check B: arr[0] = B, write_idx = 1
   Check A: skip
   Check C: arr[1] = C, write_idx = 2
   Check A: skip
   Final: [B, C, A, C, A], first 2 positions valid

First pass (count non-1):
   Check 1: skip
   Check 0: count = 1
   Check 1: skip
   Check 0: count = 2
   Check 1: skip
   count = 2

Second pass (place non-1):
   write_idx = 0
   Check 1: skip
   Check 0: arr[0] = 0, write_idx = 1
   Check 1: skip
   Check 0: arr[1] = 0, write_idx = 2
   Check 1: skip
   Final: [0, 0, 1, 0, 1], first 2 positions valid

ladders.title: Building Block — Conditional Array Traversal with Write Pointer
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array while conditionally copying elements to a write position.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on the second phase of the approach: traversing an array and selectively copying elements to specific positions using a write pointer. The write pointer starts at 0 and only advances when we encounter an element that meets our condition (in this case, not equal to x). The intuition is that we're compacting the array: we want to collect all valid elements at the front, and the write pointer tracks where the next valid element should go. As we iterate through the array, we check each element. If it's valid, we copy it to arr[write_pointer] and then increment write_pointer. If it's not valid, we simply skip it. This ensures that all valid elements end up in the first 'write_pointer' positions of the array, in their original order. The key insight is that the write pointer always points to the next available position for a valid element, and it only moves forward when we actually place an element. This is more efficient than shifting elements for each removal. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as it's the action phase of the two-pass strategy.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize write_pointer = 0
2. For each element in array:
   a. If element meets condition (e.g., != x):
        array[write_pointer] = element
        write_pointer += 1
3. The first write_pointer elements now contain all valid elements

Connection to L2: This is the implementation of the second pass in the two-pass compaction technique.

Common mistakes:
1. Incrementing write_pointer for invalid elements
2. Not copying the element before incrementing write_pointer
3. Starting write_pointer at 1 instead of 0
4. Using the same index for reading and writing

Real code pattern:
def conditional_copy(arr, x):
    write_idx = 0
    for num in arr:
        if num != x:
            arr[write_idx] = num
            write_idx += 1
    # First write_idx elements now contain non-x elements

examples.trace:
write_idx = 0
Check 1: equal to 1, skip
Check 0: not equal → arr[0] = 0, write_idx = 1
Check 1: equal to 1, skip
Check 0: not equal → arr[1] = 0, write_idx = 2
Check 1: equal to 1, skip
Result: First 2 elements [0, 0] are valid

examples.input: arr = [A, B, C, A, D], copy non-A elements
examples.output: write_idx = 3, arr becomes [B, C, D, A, D]
examples.trace:
write_idx = 0
Check A: equal to A, skip
Check B: not equal → arr[0] = B, write_idx = 1
Check C: not equal → arr[1] = C, write_idx = 2
Check A: equal to A, skip
Check D: not equal → arr[2] = D, write_idx = 3
Result: First 3 elements [B, C, D] are valid

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Comparison and Counting
ladders.desc: Compare each array element against a value and count how many match a condition.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves iterating through an array and counting elements based on a condition. It's the simplest form of filtered aggregation. The intuition is straightforward: we examine each element one by one, check if it satisfies our condition (in this case, not being equal to x), and increment a counter when it does. This operation is foundational because many algorithms need to know how many elements meet certain criteria before proceeding. The counter starts at 0 and only increases when we find elements that pass the test. This is different from simply counting all elements, which would always give the array length. The key learning is how to use a loop to examine each element and a conditional statement to decide whether to count it. Time complexity is O(n) since we examine each element once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only need the counter variable. This connects to L3 as it's the information-gathering phase that precedes the conditional copying.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. For index from 0 to array length - 1:
   a. If array[index] != x:
        count = count + 1
3. Return count

Connection to L3: This counting operation provides the information needed to know how many elements will be copied in the conditional copy phase.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to initialize count to 0
2. Using = instead of == or != in comparisons
3. Counting the wrong condition (counting x instead of non-x)
4. Not returning the count

Real code pattern:
def count_condition(arr, x):
    count = 0
    for i in range(len(arr)):
        if arr[i] != x:
            count += 1
    return count

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], count elements not equal to 3
examples.trace:
count = 0
Index 0: arr[0] = 1, 1 ≠ 3 → count = 1
Index 1: arr[1] = 2, 2 ≠ 3 → count = 2
Index 2: arr[2] = 3, 3 = 3 → skip
Index 3: arr[3] = 4, 4 ≠ 3 → count = 3
Index 4: arr[4] = 5, 5 ≠ 3 → count = 4
Return 4

examples.input: arr = [A, A, B, A, C], count elements not equal to A
examples.trace:
count = 0
Index 0: A = A → skip
Index 1: A = A → skip
Index 2: B ≠ A → count = 1
Index 3: A = A → skip
Index 4: C ≠ A → count = 2
Return 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Element Access
ladders.desc: Basic skill of looping through an array and accessing each element.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most fundamental concept required for the entire problem: how to iterate through an array and work with individual elements. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by index. The intuition is that we can examine or modify each element one by one using a loop. The most common pattern is a for-loop that goes from index 0 to length-1. At each iteration, we can access the current element using array[index]. This allows us to read its value, compare it with other values, or modify it. Understanding array traversal is crucial because almost all array algorithms build on this basic operation. Beginners should focus on two key aspects: 1) Arrays are zero-indexed (first element is at index 0), and 2) The loop should stop at length-1 because that's the last valid index. Time complexity for traversing an entire array is O(n) where n is the array length. Space complexity is O(1) for the traversal itself. This foundation connects to L4 as it's the mechanism that enables counting and comparison operations.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Get array length n
2. For index from 0 to n-1:
   a. current_element = array[index]
   b. Do something with current_element (print, compare, etc.)

Connection to L4: This basic traversal is what enables us to examine each element for the counting operation.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting loop at 1 instead of 0
2. Going up to length instead of length-1 (causes index out of bounds)
3. Confusing array values with array indices
4. Not understanding zero-based indexing

Real code pattern:
def traverse_array(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n):  # i goes from 0 to n-1
        current = arr[i]
        # Do something with current

examples.trace:
n = 4
Iteration 1: i = 0, arr[0] = 10
Iteration 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 20
Iteration 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 30
Iteration 4: i = 3, arr[3] = 40

n = 4
Iteration 1: i = 0, arr[0] = 5
Iteration 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 3
Iteration 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 7
Iteration 4: i = 3, arr[3] = 1

testCases.expected: Access arr[0]=3, arr[1]=6, arr[2]=9, arr[3]=12, arr[4]=15
Access arr[0]=9, arr[1]=8, arr[2]=7, arr[3]=6, arr[4]=5
